trade_or_storage_anchor: EU imported about 435 Mt crude in 2025; Saudi Arabia supplied 6.8%, Iraq 5.8%, and GCC countries about 7% of EU crude imports by quantity.
buffer_or_adjustment: EU/IEA emergency oil-stock framework: EU countries contribute about 20% of the IEA 400 mb coordinated release; EU also has mandatory emergency stocks and contingency plans.
interpretation: Europe is not the main Hormuz crude destination, but Mediterranean and complex EU refineries have real sour-crude exposure; the bigger EU-wide effect is Brent/product price pass-through.
caveats: Iraq is not GCC but much of southern Iraqi export exposure is Hormuz-linked; country crude-origin shares do not identify port/loading route or refinery-specific crude slates.

region: EU Mediterranean refineries and products market
exposure_class: indirect price, with localized direct physical risk
trade_or_storage_anchor: IEA says Gulf producers exported 3.3 mb/d refined oil products and 1.5 mb/d LPG in 2025; nearly 3 mb/d of regional refining capacity has been shut. The European Commission identified jet fuel as the main near-term EU product constraint.
buffer_or_adjustment: Commission advised deferring non-emergency refinery maintenance, preserving free product flows, and considering voluntary fuel-saving measures; EU refining can partly substitute, but feedstock and margin constraints bind.
interpretation: The physical loss is not mainly EU-bound Gulf product cargoes; it is a global middle-distillate/jet-fuel balance shock that lands in Europe through wholesale prices, aviation fuel, diesel, freight, and refinery crude-slate constraints.
caveats: Public sources do not provide a clean EU import-by-product-from-Hormuz table; classify as price/local availability exposure, not a de-duplicated volume loss.

trade_or_storage_anchor: Eurostat says Qatar supplied 8.9% of EU LNG imports by value in 2025; Commission Q2 2025 gas report gives Qatar 8% of EU LNG, 2.7 bcm, in that quarter; ACER says winter 2025/26 Qatar LNG was 7% of EU LNG and 4% of total EU gas imports.
buffer_or_adjustment: GIE/S49 notes show EU underground gas storage at 565.62 TWh and 50.03% full on 2026-07-05, about 56.7 days of average 2025 demand; ACER says 80% storage is feasible at current LNG import rates but 90% is difficult without extra supply.
interpretation: Europe has a direct Qatar LNG cargo exposure, but Asia is more exposed. Europe is still highly exposed to global LNG price competition because it has become a large flexible LNG buyer after the loss of Russian pipeline gas.
caveats: Qatar share is modest relative to US LNG, Norway pipeline gas, and total EU gas; storage buffers volume but not refill cost. LNG cargo destination data and contract flexibility are partly proprietary.

trade_or_storage_anchor: No UK-specific Hormuz import share was collected in this pass; exposure is through TTF/NBP-linked gas, Brent/product benchmarks, insurance, freight, and competition for Atlantic Basin LNG cargoes.
buffer_or_adjustment: Storage and import-terminal flexibility differ by country; the EU GIE storage row is not a direct UK buffer estimate.
interpretation: Classify as indirect price exposure unless a country-level cargo/import table shows material Qatari/Gulf direct flows.
caveats: Out of scope for this pass to build a UK/non-EU national import matrix; needs UK DESNZ/HMRC and terminal data if it becomes blog-critical.

exposure_class: direct physical likely, but not quantified here
trade_or_storage_anchor: Türkiye/eastern Mediterranean countries are geographically closer to Iraqi, Saudi, Qatari, and Gulf product flows than northwest Europe, but a harmonized 2025 official source matrix was not completed in this issue.
buffer_or_adjustment: Adjustment likely combines Russian/Azeri/Mediterranean crude, product imports, Turkish Straits/Black Sea flows, LNG spot cargoes, and demand response.
interpretation: Treat as a candidate high-priority follow-up: Mediterranean exposure is probably more physical than the EU aggregate, but current evidence in this issue is insufficient for a numeric claim.
caveats: Do not publish a Turkey/eastern Med share without EMRA/national customs/UN Comtrade reconciliation; some Iraqi crude may route via Ceyhan rather than Hormuz when northern flows are available.

exposure_class: mostly indirect price; limited direct physical
trade_or_storage_anchor: FAO says Hormuz normally carries up to 30% of internationally traded fertilizers and nearly half of global sulfur trade; World Bank says urea prices were above $850/mt in April 2026, up 80% since February, and Middle East disruptions drove the fertilizer index higher.
buffer_or_adjustment: S49.5 finds no global fertilizer strategic reserve; Europe can substitute through Morocco, Egypt, Russia/non-Russia suppliers, existing seasonal purchasing, and lower application rates, but those are cost/demand adjustments rather than clean stock buffers.
interpretation: Europe is exposed mainly through fertilizer and food-production input prices, not because the EU is the dominant direct destination for Gulf fertilizer cargoes.
caveats: Public EU fertilizer import-origin data by product was not fully collected here; do not overstate direct physical dependence. Fertilizer, ammonia, sulfur, and DAP rows overlap through feedstock chains.

exposure_class: mostly indirect price, with selective direct supply-chain exposure
trade_or_storage_anchor: IEA says the steepest losses have occurred in petrochemicals because feedstock availability is constrained; it also says around 5 Mt/year of Gulf aluminium ships through Hormuz and Gulf producers exported 1.5 mb/d LPG in 2025.
buffer_or_adjustment: Adjustment is mainly price rationing, alternative feedstocks/origins, inventory draw, and delayed industrial production; public petrochemical inventory data are weak.
interpretation: This is a real industrial-cost channel for European chemicals, plastics, packaging, metals, and fertilizer chains, but not yet a clean direct-import-volume story.
caveats: Avoid summing LPG, petrochemicals, sulfur, fertilizer, and aluminium because they overlap in feedstocks and downstream uses; petrochemical cargo-flow data likely require ICIS/Argus/Kpler/Bloomberg-type sources.

trade_or_storage_anchor: ACER reports EU gas demand depends more than 90% on imports and LNG reached 47% of total gas supply in 2025; ACER winter 2025/26 notes TTF price spikes and higher price volatility from Hormuz/LNG competition.
buffer_or_adjustment: Gas storage and demand reduction provide a physical buffer, but storage refill happens at market prices; power exposure varies with hydro/wind/nuclear availability and gas-fired marginal generation.
interpretation: The most defensible Europe-wide consumer channel is gas and electricity price volatility rather than a direct outage from missing Hormuz cargoes.
caveats: Power-market pass-through requires country-level generation mix and tariff/regulatory analysis; not quantified in this issue.
